In the Valley of Shifting Sands lived Anoki, a coyote who walked on two legs. Anoki approached the village weavers with a dazzling offer: he would spin their leftover straw into "Moon-Thread," a silk so fine it could catch dreams, but only if they gave him their firstborn lambs as payment upfront. The weavers, desperate for glory, agreed. Anoki took the lambs, feasted, and fled, leaving behind only bundles of glittering spiderwebs that dissolved at dawn.

The betrayal was perfect, until the Wind Spirit, who sees all, heard the weavers’ weeping. The Wind did not strike Anoki with lightning, for that is cheap. Instead, she cursed him with heavy pockets. Every time Anoki stole or cheated, gold coins would materialize in his fur, weighing him down.

At first, Anoki rejoiced, but the gold could not be removed or spent. He grew slower. When he tried to flee his next con, the weight anchored him to the earth. The punishment was costly for the Wind, too; she had to sacrifice the clouds' silver lining to forge the gold. Finally, Anoki, crushed under his own greed, surrendered. He could no longer run or trick, only sit still as a golden statue, a warning that the weight of deceit eventually breaks the deceiver's back. The cycle ended, for a statue cannot lie.

But Shamash, the sun god who sees all, watched from above. He saw the stolen grain and the lies. Annoyed by Zair’s arrogance, Shamash did not strike him dead. Instead, he cursed Zair with the Midas touch—but only for things he truly loved. When Zair embraced his gold, it turned to ash. When he ate his feast, it became dust.

The punishment was costly for the city, too. To keep Zair from starving and his curse from spreading, the people had to feed him by hand, treating the thief like an invalid king. The cycle ended when Zair, broken by dependency and guilt, wept true tears. His sorrow washed away the curse, leaving him a humble servant, forever bound to repay the kindness of those he once deceived.

Myth: In the coastal city of Tyre, lived Liora, a merchant who sold bottled storms. She claimed her glass vials held the furious winds of the north, promising sailors calm seas if they released her "storms" on their rivals. To prove it, she paid local urchins to sabotage the ships of those who refused her wares. The captains, fearing her power, paid her tribute in pearls.

But Yam, the god of the sea, felt the false currents. Insulted by Liora’s meddling, he did not drown her. Instead, he cursed her with a voice of thunder. Whenever she whispered a lie or a threat, a hurricane roared from her lips, destroying her own shop and goods.

The punishment was costly for the city. To prevent Liora from leveling the market with a casual word, the citizens had to build her a silent tower of thick stone and bring her offerings in total silence. The cycle ended not with her death, but with her silence. Liora, realizing her voice was now a weapon she could not control, took a vow of eternal quiet. She became the city’s silent guardian, watching the waves for real storms, her greed washed away by the terrible power of her own words.

The proud eagles agreed, stripping themselves bare. Kaelo laughed, stole their discarded plumage to make himself a magnificent cloak, and left the flightless eagles stranded on the cold rocks.

At first, Kaelo preened, looking like a king. But when he tried to fly, he plummeted like a stone, shattering the earth beneath him. The punishment was costly for the Mountain; to sustain the curse, she had to drain the vitality from her own wildflowers. Kaelo, unable to soar and trapped by his own vanity, became a flightless prisoner of the peaks. The cycle ended, for a bird that cannot fly can no longer look down on others.
